subsystem: Exiv2 HTTP client transport. This is the free-function Exiv2::http(), the minimal blocking/non-blocking HTTP/1.x client that Exiv2 uses to fetch remote resources (e.g. images/metadata over http:// and via HTTP_PROXY). It takes a request Dictionary (server, page, verb, header, version, port), opens a TCP socket, formats and sends a raw request line/headers, then reads the server's response, splits headers from body, extracts the status code, and returns the parsed body in response["body"].
provenance: Identified from the Exiv2 namespace and library types: Exiv2::Dictionary, Exiv2::Uri / Exiv2::Uri::Parse, the stringToDict/error/forgive/flushBuffer/makeNonBlocking helpers, the global httpTemplate format string and blankLines table, and the OK()/lengthof() macros — these are the internal helpers of Exiv2's src/http.cpp. The leading comment `/* sample: primevul_train_2587_vuln */` and the file name eval_0010_cwe476.cpp corroborate that this is the vulnerable revision of that function.
trust_boundary: Two untrusted producers cross into this function. (1) THE HTTP RESPONSE bytes read from the remote peer at lines 138 and 189 are fully attacker-controlled network data: a malicious or MITM'd server picks every byte, including whether the response contains a space or a newline, how long the status/header block is, and where blank-line separators fall. All parsing at lines 139-196 runs on this hostile buffer. (2) THE REQUEST Dictionary (server/page/header/verb/version/port) and the HTTP_PROXY/NO_PROXY environment variables are at best semi-trusted — in Exiv2 the server/page/URL frequently originate from a URL embedded in a file being parsed, so their length and content are influenced by the attacker who supplies the image. The response is the primary hostile surface; the request fields are the secondary one that drives the request-formatting overflow.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the request and response dictionaries by reference plus an errors out-string, and returns an int (the HTTP-ish result/status). The first block fills in defaults for any missing request keys: verb defaults to GET, header to empty, version to 1.0, port to empty. request is an Exiv2::Dictionary (a std::map<std::string,std::string>); note that these writes mutate request, which matters later because raw c_str() pointers into this same map are cached at lines 24-29.

Local state: file accumulates the response body, errors is cleared, and result holds the return value (initialized 0). On Windows the socket stack is initialized with WSAStartup. The rest of the function then uses the Winsock names (SOCKET_ERROR, WSAGetLastError, closesocket, Sleep) which on POSIX are supplied by Exiv2 compatibility shims.

Raw C-string pointers are cached from the request map entries. Because request["server"] etc. use operator[], any key not already present is default-inserted here (server/page were not defaulted above, so they are created empty if absent). servername_p/port_p are working copies that may later be redirected to the proxy host/port. url is the full request URL, built by concatenating http://, server and page into a fresh std::string that lives for the rest of the function; page is later repointed to url.c_str() when a proxy is used (line 60).

Proxy discovery: the HTTP_PROXY / http_proxy environment variables are read. bProx is true if either is set; prox picks the lowercase form first, else the uppercase, else empty. The chosen proxy URL string is parsed by Exiv2::Uri::Parse into a Uri struct (with Host and Port members used below). This is the standard proxy-from-environment convention.

The NO_PROXY / no_proxy environment variables are read the same way, then turned (with localhost and 127.0.0.1 appended) into a Dictionary by stringToDict. If the target servername is a key in that no-proxy set, bProx is cleared so the proxy is bypassed. noProxy.count(servername) implicitly constructs a std::string from the servername char* for the lookup.

When a proxy is in effect, the connection target is redirected: servername_p/port_p point at the proxy's Host/Port and the request path page is switched to the absolute url (proxies require the absolute-URI form). Note Proxy.Host/Port c_str() pointers are valid because Proxy is a local that outlives the function body. Finally, empty ports default to 80 for both the direct and proxy paths.

A TCP/IPv4 socket is created; on failure the function returns via error(). A sockaddr_in is zeroed, then filled: sin_addr from inet_addr(servername_p) (parses a dotted-quad; returns INADDR_NONE for names), family AF_INET, and sin_port from htons(atoi(port_p)) converting the port string to a network-order 16-bit port. atoi of a non-numeric port yields 0.

If inet_addr failed (INADDR_NONE), the servername is treated as a hostname and resolved with gethostbyname. A null result means unknown host and the function closes the socket and returns an error (servername_p is passed as an argument to error, not as the format). Otherwise the first resolved address is copied into sin_addr with a fixed 4-byte memcpy sized by sizeof(serv_addr.sin_addr).

The socket is set non-blocking, then connect() is attempted. Because the socket is non-blocking, an immediate EWOULDBLOCK/WSAEWOULDBLOCK is expected and tolerated; any other error closes the socket and returns via error() with a proper printf-style format string and the servername/port/errorCode as variadic arguments.

A 32 KiB+1 stack buffer is declared; buff_l is one less (32768), reserving room for a terminator. The request line/headers are formatted into buffer with snprintf using the global httpTemplate and the caller-derived verb/page/version/servername/header strings. snprintf returns n = the number of characters that WOULD have been written (excluding the NUL) had the buffer been unbounded — NOT the number actually written. The code then writes buffer[n] = 0 and constructs a std::string of length n, both trusting n as an in-bounds index/length.

The formatted request (n bytes) is sent in a retry loop: while there is still time budget (sleep_ >= 0) and send fails, it naps for snooze ms and decrements the budget, effectively waiting for the non-blocking connection to complete. If the budget runs out (sleep_ < 0), the sockets are closed and a timeout error is returned. sleep_ and snooze are module-level globals.

Response reading begins. end is the running write offset into buffer, bSearching indicates the header block has not yet been located, status holds the parsed HTTP status (default 200). The first recv reads up to buff_l bytes at the buffer start; forgive() normalizes transient errors (e.g. EWOULDBLOCK) into a non-fatal count. The loop continues while n>=0 and OK(status). On a non-zero read, end advances by n and buffer[end] is NUL-terminated so the buffer can be treated as a C string. Because each recv is bounded by buff_l - end (see line 189), end stays <= buff_l, so buffer[end] stays within the +1 slack byte.

While still searching, the code scans for any known blank-line separator (blankLines holds the CRLFCRLF / LFLF variants) with strstr. When found, bSearching is cleared and body is set to the byte offset just past the separator — the start of the response body. status is then parsed by finding the first space in buffer (the space after 'HTTP/1.1') and running atoi on it, i.e. atoi(strchr(buffer, ' ')). This assumes the response contains a space.

Header parsing. Leading newlines are skipped, then h is moved past the first '\n' (the end of the status line) via strchr(...)+1; the status line text is stored in response[""]. result is again parsed as atoi(strchr(buffer,' ')). Then, for each subsequent line up to body, the code locates the ':' and the '\n', splits key = text before ':', value = text after ':' up to the newline, and stores response[key]=value. The inner-loop guards (c && first_newline && c < first_newline && h < buffer+body) do check for NULL from the per-iteration strchr calls — but the FIRST strchr at line 162 and the atoi(strchr(...)) at 164 are NOT NULL-checked.

If the buffer is nearly full while still searching for headers, searching is abandoned (treat as no headers). Once headers are located, flushBuffer copies buffer[body..end] into the file accumulator. The next recv appends at buffer+end for up to buff_l-end bytes, keeping end <= buff_l. A zero read naps and decrements the time budget; if it expires n is set to the FINISH sentinel to break the loop. Note flushBuffer does not reset end, so once the header block is fixed the client can buffer at most ~32 KiB of response before the appends saturate and reads return 0 — a functional cap, not an overflow.

Finalization. If the loop ended abnormally (not FINISH, or a non-OK status), a diagnostic is formatted into buffer with sizeof buffer (32769, the full size — safe here) and passed to error(); note buffer is passed as error()'s 2nd argument, so error() must not treat it as a printf format lest a caller-influenced status/error code with '%' become a format-string bug. If the transfer finished cleanly but no headers were ever found, any buffered bytes are flushed as the body; if nothing at all was received, an error is returned.

The sockets are closed, the assembled body is stored in response["body"], and the parsed result (HTTP status) is returned. Note both closesocket(server) and closesocket(sockfd) are called even though server is the return value of connect() (0 or SOCKET_ERROR on POSIX, not the descriptor) — closing an integer status rather than a descriptor, a latent resource/handle-hygiene smell rather than a security bug here.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: buffer holds the raw response the remote server sent. strchr(buffer, ' ') returns NULL if there is no space character anywhere in what has been received so far (e.g. a response line 'HTTP/1.1200OK', a body/fragment with no spaces yet, or a hostile first packet deliberately containing none). atoi(NULL) then dereferences a NULL pointer. The attacker fully controls these bytes, so a single crafted response reliably reaches this line (a blank-line separator was just matched, so control is here) and crashes the process — a remote NULL-pointer-dereference denial of service. Invariant: the result of strchr must be NULL-checked before it is passed to atoi/dereferenced.

N is '\n'. If the received buffer contains no newline (a server can send a status line with no terminating LF, or trickle bytes so the first recv has none), strchr returns NULL and the code computes NULL + 1, producing an invalid pointer. Line 163 then does std::string(buffer + i).substr(0, h - buffer - 2), using h in pointer arithmetic and as an implicit bound; with h == (char*)1 the length expression h - buffer - 2 becomes a wildly negative/huge value fed to substr, and h is later dereferenced by strchr(h, ...). This is a NULL/invalid-pointer dereference (CWE-476) and a potential out-of-bounds read, both driven entirely by attacker response bytes. Invariant: reject/verify the presence of the status-line newline before advancing h.

Identical hazard to line 151: strchr(buffer, ' ') can be NULL when the response contains no space, and atoi immediately dereferences it. This is the value ultimately returned to the caller as the HTTP result, and it is parsed without any NULL guard. A malicious server that omits the space after the HTTP version crashes the client here. Invariant: guard strchr before atoi.

snprintf returns the number of characters that WOULD have been written had the buffer been large enough, not the number actually written. If the formatted request (verb + page/url + version + servername + header) is >= buff_l (32768) — reachable when the caller-supplied server/page/header, e.g. from an attacker-influenced URL, are long — n exceeds the buffer and buffer[n] = 0 performs an out-of-bounds write past the 32769-byte stack buffer (stack corruption, CWE-787). std::string(buffer, n) then reads n bytes, an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) that also copies adjacent stack memory into response["requestheaders"]. Additionally, on an encoding error snprintf returns a NEGATIVE value, making buffer[n] a write before the buffer. Invariant: clamp n to [0, buff_l-1] (or reject when n<0 || (size_t)n>=buff_l) before indexing or constructing the string.

The copy trusts that host->h_addr points to at least 4 bytes. gethostbyname is checked for NULL (line 88) but host->h_addr itself, and h_length, are not validated. For a well-behaved resolver returning an AF_INET record this is fine (h_length == 4), but a malicious or misconfigured name service / hosts database returning a record with a shorter address list or unexpected family could make this a short/OOB read. Latent, low severity and dependent on the resolver, but the fixed 4-byte assumption is unchecked. Invariant: verify host->h_addrtype == AF_INET and host->h_length >= sizeof(sin_addr) before the memcpy.

buffer is formatted from a fixed literal template and only integer arguments, so it cannot itself contain attacker '%' specifiers — this call is safe as written. The concern is contract-level: buffer is passed as error()'s second parameter, the same slot that elsewhere (lines 90, 103, 127, 210) receives a printf-style FORMAT string. If error() forwards that argument to a printf-family function as the format, then any code path that ever routes attacker-controlled text through that slot would be a CWE-134 format-string bug. Here the text is controlled, so this is an info-level note to confirm error()'s handling, not an exploitable finding in this file.

summary: This is Exiv2's HTTP client http(). The dominant risk is remote NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476): the response parser repeatedly does atoi(strchr(buffer,' ')) and strchr(buffer,'\n')+1 with no NULL check, so a malicious server that omits a space or newline crashes the client. A second, distinct memory-safety bug is the request formatter trusting snprintf's return value (buffer[n]=0 and std::string(buffer,n)), which is an out-of-bounds write/read when the request exceeds 32 KiB or snprintf errors. Both are attacker-reachable; the NULL derefs are the easiest to trigger.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int Exiv2::http(Dictionary& request, Dictionary& response, std::string& errors) (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Signature itself; request/response are the trust-boundary carriers, analyzed via their field uses below.
Sets safe string defaults; no length/bound contract of its own.
Pointers into the request map; request is not mutated after this point, so no dangling/UAF; their content feeds the snprintf finding.
Local string that outlives its later c_str() use at line 60; no lifetime bug.
Environment-derived proxy config; not remote-attacker data in the primary model; parsed by Uri::Parse/stringToDict (out of file).
Proxy is a local outliving its c_str() use; parsing logic is not in this file.
Map lookup with implicit std::string construction from a valid c_str; bounded and safe.
Standard socket create with error check.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: struct sockaddr_in serv_addr / int serv_len = sizeof(serv_addr) / memset (lines 76-78)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size kernel struct, correctly sized and zeroed.
Scalar conversions of the target address/port; no buffer contract.
Fixed 4-byte memcpy trusts h_addr/h_length without checking h_addrtype/h_length — latent short/OOB read.
error() uses a literal format with variadic args; not a format-string bug.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char buffer[32 * 1024 + 1] / size_t buff_l = sizeof buffer - 1 (lines 107-108)
reviewed_anchors.reason: The 32 KiB stack destination buffer; its indexing by snprintf's return value is unsafe (see line 113).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int n = snprintf(buffer, buff_l, httpTemplate, ...); buffer[n] = 0; std::string(buffer, n) (lines 112-114)
reviewed_anchors.reason: snprintf return value used as index/length without clamping — OOB write/read; negative on error.
Format is a fixed global literal; user data is in the variadic arguments — not CWE-134.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: send() retry loop and timeout error() (lines 118-128)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Literal format strings; sends exactly n bytes; no new memory contract beyond the snprintf finding.
Each recv is bounded by buff_l-end (line 189) so end <= buff_l; buffer[end] stays within the +1 slack byte — no overflow.
Initial read bounded by buff_l into the buffer start; safe.
strstr on a NUL-terminated buffer; body offset bounded by the match; used as a start-of-body marker.
strchr may return NULL (no space in response) and is dereferenced by atoi — remote NULL deref.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: h = strchr(h + i, N) + 1 and std::string(buffer + i).substr(0, h - buffer - 2) (lines 162-163)
reviewed_anchors.reason: strchr may be NULL; NULL+1 then used in pointer arithmetic/substr — NULL deref / OOB read.
Same unchecked strchr-then-atoi NULL deref as line 151.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: header parse while (c && first_newline && c < first_newline && h < buffer + body) (lines 165-176)
reviewed_anchors.reason: This inner loop DOES NULL-check c and first_newline each iteration and bounds h by buffer+body; substr lengths are non-negative given the c<first_newline guard.
Guard runs while end < buff_l, so buff_l-end does not underflow; abandons header search near buffer-full.
Consumes buffer[body..end]; body<=end and end<=buff_l hold; implementation not shown but bounds passed are in range.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: n = forgive(recv(sockfd, buffer + end, (int)(buff_l - end), 0), err) (line 189)
reviewed_anchors.reason: end <= buff_l invariant keeps buff_l-end non-negative and the append within the buffer; no underflow/overflow.
Uses full sizeof buffer and only integer args; buffer content is a fixed template with no attacker '%'; safe as written.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: closesocket(server); closesocket(sockfd); response["body"] = file; return result (lines 217-220)
reviewed_anchors.reason: server is connect()'s status (not a descriptor); closing it is a hygiene smell, not a security bug.

findings.title: Remote NULL-pointer dereference: atoi(strchr(buffer,' ')) on unchecked response
findings.invariant: The pointer returned by strchr/strstr on attacker-controlled response bytes must be NULL-checked before it is dereferenced or handed to atoi.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious or MITM'd HTTP server returns a response with no space character in the received bytes (e.g. a status line without the space after the HTTP version, or a short/spaceless first packet). strchr(buffer,' ') returns NULL and atoi(NULL) dereferences a NULL pointer, crashing the Exiv2 process — a reliable remote denial of service. The identical pattern recurs at line 164 (result = atoi(strchr(buffer,' '))).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no NULL guard between strchr and atoi at lines 151 and 164, and that buffer at this point is purely the remote-supplied response.

findings.title: NULL/invalid pointer dereference: strchr(...,'\n')+1 used without NULL check
findings.code:
h = strchr(h + i, N) + 1;
                response[""] = std::string(buffer + i).substr(0, h - buffer - 2);
findings.bug_class: NULL pointer dereference + out-of-bounds read (pointer arithmetic on NULL)
findings.invariant: The status-line newline must be found and verified non-NULL before h is advanced; the substr length (h - buffer - 2) must be a validated non-negative bound.
findings.failure_mode: If the response contains no '\n' (server sends a status line with no terminating LF, or the first recv delivers bytes without a newline yet), strchr returns NULL and the code forms NULL+1 == (char*)1. That bogus pointer is used at line 163 in std::string(...).substr(0, h - buffer - 2) — producing a nonsensical/huge length passed to substr — and is later dereferenced by strchr(h, C) at line 165. Result is a crash and/or out-of-bounds read on attacker-controlled input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm strchr(h+i, N) can be NULL for a truncated/newline-less response and that h is used unguarded at lines 163 and 165.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds write/read: snprintf return value used as buffer index and string length
findings.anchor: int n = snprintf(buffer, buff_l, httpTemplate, ...); buffer[n] = 0;
findings.bug_class: Stack out-of-bounds write / out-of-bounds read (misuse of snprintf return value)
findings.invariant: snprintf's return value is the would-be length, not the written length; before using it as an index/length it must be clamped: reject n < 0 and treat n >= buff_l as truncation (index at most buff_l-1).
findings.failure_mode: When the formatted request (verb + page/absolute-url + version + servername + header, all caller/URL-derived and attacker-influenceable) reaches or exceeds buff_l (32768), snprintf returns n >= 32768. buffer[n] = 0 then writes a zero byte past the 32769-byte stack buffer (CWE-787 stack corruption), and std::string(buffer, n) reads n bytes past the buffer (CWE-125), leaking adjacent stack memory into response["requestheaders"]. On a snprintf encoding error n is negative, making buffer[n] a write before the buffer. Impact ranges from crash to stack memory disclosure/corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm httpTemplate plus the concatenated caller fields can exceed 32768 bytes (e.g. a long server/page/header from a crafted URL) and that n is used unclamped at lines 113-114.

findings.title: Unchecked gethostbyname address copy (fixed 4-byte memcpy trusts h_addr/h_length)
findings.invariant: Before copying, verify host->h_addrtype == AF_INET and host->h_length >= sizeof(sin_addr), and that host->h_addr is non-NULL.
findings.failure_mode: gethostbyname is checked for NULL, but host->h_addr and h_length are not. A malicious or misconfigured name-resolution source returning an unexpected address family/length (or an empty h_addr_list) could cause a short/out-of-bounds read of the source. This is a latent, low-severity, resolver-dependent issue; for a normal AF_INET record h_length == 4 and the copy is correct.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the resolver path can return records where h_length != 4 or h_addr is not backed by at least 4 bytes; add a length/type guard.

audit_checklist: Add NULL checks to every strchr/strstr result on the response buffer before atoi/dereference (lines 151, 162, 164) — the primary remote-DoS surface.
Clamp the snprintf return value at line 112: reject n < 0 and cap n at buff_l-1 before buffer[n]=0 and std::string(buffer,n).
Verify the request-line length bound: can attacker-influenced server/page/header/URL make the formatted request exceed 32768 bytes?

Confirm error()'s second parameter is never used as a printf format with attacker-controlled text (it is a literal format at lines 90/103/127/210 and a controlled buffer at 201 — verify the implementation).
Validate host->h_addrtype/h_length before the sizeof(sin_addr) memcpy at line 92.
Confirm the end/buff_l-end invariant truly bounds every recv (lines 138, 189) and buffer[end] index (line 142) so no path lets end exceed buff_l.
Review flushBuffer's handling of (buffer, body, end) bounds and whether not resetting end can strand/duplicate body data (functional correctness).
Check that closesocket(server) at lines 208/217 is intended given server is connect()'s return status, not a descriptor.
